title: Generalization: The Real Goal of Machine Learning
concept: A model's true purpose isn't nailing the data it already saw — it's generalizing: performing well on brand-new data it will meet in the real world.

exercises.prompt: Recap: A model that's too complex tends to memorize noise instead of the real pattern. What's that failure called?

options.text: Overfitting
Underfitting
Validating

exercises.explain: Right! Now let's name the actual goal every model is chasing — the thing overfitting and underfitting both get in the way of.

exercises.prompt: True or false: The whole point of splitting data into train, validation, and test is ultimately to check how well a model handles data it hasn't seen before.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: True! Every split, every check — it all exists to answer one question: will this work on new data?

cards.title: Two driving students
cards.body: Student A practices only in one empty parking lot, over and over, until the turns feel automatic. Student B practices on many different streets — busy roads, tight corners, rain, night driving. On test day, in an unfamiliar neighborhood, only one of them is actually ready.

cards.title: What is generalization?
cards.body: Generalization is a model's ability to perform well on new, unseen data — not just the exact examples it trained on. It's the real goal of training: not to memorize, but to learn something that transfers.

cards.title: The chef who understands technique
cards.body: A chef who only knows 5 exact recipes from school is stuck the moment an ingredient is missing. A chef who understands why a sauce thickens or a dough rises can improvise with almost any new ingredient. Real learning generalizes; rote memorization doesn't.

cards.title: A self-driving car meets new weather
cards.body: A self-driving model trained only on sunny California roads may drive beautifully there — but the real test is dropping it into rainy Seattle traffic it has never encountered. If it still performs well, it has generalized. If it falls apart, it only ever memorized California.

cards.title: Nailing the training data isn't the finish line
cards.body: It's tempting to treat a perfect training score as success. But training data is just practice material — the real finish line is how the model handles situations it has never met, out in the real world.

left.text:
Nails the exact training examples
Falls apart on anything new

right.text:
Learned the real pattern
Performs well on new situations

exercises.prompt: What is generalization, in a machine learning model?

options.text: The ability to perform well on new data it has never seen before
The ability to memorize the training data perfectly
The speed at which a model trains
The number of features a model uses

exercises.explain: Exactly! Generalization is about handling new situations well, not memorizing old ones.

exercises.prompt: True or false: The real goal of training a model is to score as high as possible on the training data.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: False! The training score is just a means to an end — the real goal is generalizing well to new, unseen data.

exercises.sentence: A model that ___ well can handle new situations it never trained on.
exercises.explain: Generalizing means the model learned something that transfers to new, unseen situations.

exercises.prompt: Which of these shows a model that has genuinely generalized?

options.text: A handwriting model trained on thousands of different people's handwriting correctly reads a brand-new person's handwriting it has never seen
A handwriting model perfectly reads the exact training samples it memorized, but fails on anyone else's handwriting
A handwriting model that hasn't been trained on any data at all

exercises.explain: Correct! Performing well on a genuinely new person's handwriting is real evidence of generalization.

exercises.prompt: Why do we ultimately care about train/validation/test splits and about avoiding both underfitting and overfitting?

options.text: All of it is in service of one goal: a model that generalizes well to new, real-world data
Because more data splits always look more impressive on a report
Because it's required by law for every model
Because it makes the training data itself smaller and faster to store

exercises.explain: Exactly! Every technique this unit covers — the training loop, avoiding over/underfitting, validation — all exists to serve generalization.

exercises.prompt: Two fruit-sorting models both score 97% on their training photos. Model A scores 95% on new fruit photos. Model B scores 40% on new fruit photos. Which model actually achieved the real goal of machine learning, and why?

options.text: Model A — it generalized, performing well on new data, which is the actual goal, not just the training score
Model B — it's fine, since both scored the same on training data
Both equally — training score is all that matters
Neither — a 97% training score is a failure regardless of anything else

exercises.explain: Right! Model A's strong score on brand-new photos shows real generalization — the true goal. Model B's training score was hiding an overfitting problem.

exercises.prompt: A hiring manager says: "Our resume-screening model is amazing — it got 100% on every resume in our historical dataset." What's the most important follow-up question, based on what generalization actually means?

options.text: "How does it perform on new resumes it has never seen before?" — since that's the real measure of whether it generalizes
"How fast did it reach 100%?" — since speed is what matters most
No follow-up needed — 100% on historical data proves it's ready for real use
"Can we remove the test set to save storage space?" — since it already scored perfectly

exercises.explain: Exactly! A perfect score on data it already saw says nothing about generalization — the only question that matters is how it handles brand-new resumes.
